The Latest Breakthroughs in Smart Textile Applications
======================================================

3 Apr 2024 |

[E-Textile & Smart Textile](https://textilesinside.com/markets/e-textile-smart-textile/)

 In 2023, a wave of innovations swept through the smart textile industry, marked by groundbreaking advancements from global research institutions. These developments herald a new era in the integration of technology and textiles, offering transformative potential across various sectors. 

From WSU’s electrically conductive, cotton-like fibres to MIT’s temperature-responsive FibeRobo, innovative breakthroughs are reshaping the landscape of [smart textiles](https://textilesinside.com/e-textile-smart-textile/). These advancements, such as Singapore’s spider-inspired durable fibres and Canada’s heat and electricity-activated materials, highlight the versatility of this rapidly evolving field. These developments not only enhance textile functionality but also open new avenues in healthcare, automotive, and fashion industries, blending traditional craftsmanship with cutting-edge technology.

WSU’s Pioneering Cotton-Like Fibre
----------------------------------

A breakthrough in smart textile innovation emerges from [Washington State University](https://news.wsu.edu/press-release/2023/12/11/new-conductive-cotton-based-fiber-developed-for-smart-textiles/) (WSU), where researchers have developed a single strand of fiber combining the flexibility of cotton with the electric conductivity of polyaniline polymer. Published in the journal Carbohydrate Polymers, their study showcases the potential of these fibers for wearable [e-textiles](https://textilesinside.com/e-textile-smart-textile/). Led by Hang Liu, the WSU team envisions integrating these fibers into apparel as sensor patches with flexible circuits, catering to diverse applications such as hazardous exposure detection for firefighters or health monitoring in everyday clothing.

Overcoming challenges in mixing the conductive polymer with cotton cellulose, the researchers devised a method involving dissolving recycled t-shirt cellulose and the conductive polymer separately before merging them side-by-side. This delicate balance at the interface ensures optimal bonding without compromising conductivity. Supported by the National Science Foundation and the Walmart Foundation Project, this research underscores the transformative role of interdisciplinary collaboration in advancing smart textile technologies.

MIT’s Shape-Changing FibeRobo
-----------------------------

MIT researchers have unveiled [FibeRobo](https://news.mit.edu/2023/shape-shifting-fiber-can-produce-morphing-fabrics-1026), a programmable fiber poised to revolutionize textile manufacturing and wearable technology. This low-cost fiber exhibits remarkable shape-changing capabilities, contracting in response to temperature changes and reverting without embedded sensors or hard components. Compatible with existing textile manufacturing techniques, including weaving looms and industrial knitting machines, FibeRobo offers endless possibilities for adaptive performance wear and smart garments.

By incorporating conductive thread, it can be activated electrically, enabling digital control over fabric form. Jack Forman, the lead author, highlights the fiber’s potential to transform everyday textiles into responsive and dynamic materials, seamlessly integrating functionality into fashion. Through meticulous fabrication processes, the team produced FibeRobo at a fraction of the cost of commercially available alternatives. With applications ranging from adaptive sports bras to compression jackets for pets, this groundbreaking fiber heralds a new era of interactive and versatile textiles. Collaborators from MIT and Northeastern University contributed to this interdisciplinary research, supported by various grants and fellowships.

Singapore’s Spiders-Inspired Soft Fibres
----------------------------------------

Taking inspiration from spiders’ silk-spinning process, researchers at the [National University of Singapore](https://news.nus.edu.sg/nus-researchers-create-method-to-produce-soft-recyclable-fibres/) (NUS) have developed an innovative method to produce soft, recyclable fibers for smart textiles. This method, known as phase separation-enabled ambient (PSEA) spinning, allows for the creation of strong, stretchable, and electrically conductive fibers at room temperature and pressure. By mimicking the natural process of spider silk formation, the team successfully integrated three key properties into the fibers: strength, elasticity, and electrical conductivity.

These fibers, made from a gel solution of polyacrylonitrile (PAN) and silver ions, demonstrate promising applications in smart technology, including interactive gaming gloves and temperature-sensing face masks. Moreover, the environmentally friendly aspect of these fibers lies in their recyclability, as they can be dissolved in a common solvent and reused to produce new fibers. This breakthrough offers a sustainable and efficient solution for the production of functional soft fibers in various smart applications.

Canadian Innovation: Heat and Electricity-Activated Materials
-------------------------------------------------------------

Researchers at the [University of Waterloo](https://uwaterloo.ca/news/media/new-programmable-smart-fabric-responds-temperature-and) have developed a groundbreaking smart material that responds to both heat and electricity, marking the first of its kind to do so. This innovative fabric opens doors to various applications, including self-warming clothing and resilient vehicle bumpers. Made from polymer nano-composite fibers sourced from recycled plastic, the fabric can change color and shape upon stimulation. Dr. Milad Kamkar, a chemical engineering professor at Waterloo, highlights its potential in AI, robotics, and virtual reality experiences.

The fabric’s design blends soft and hard materials, utilizing highly engineered polymer composites and stainless steel in its woven structure. Moreover, the fabric can be activated with lower voltage, enhancing energy efficiency and enabling integration into smaller devices like biomedical tools and environmental sensors. Inspired by biomimicry, the material’s ability to sense and react to environmental stimuli offers possibilities for eco-friendly monitoring applications. Moving forward, researchers aim to enhance the fabric’s shape-memory capabilities for use in robotic systems, envisioning versatile robots capable of carrying and transferring weight efficiently.

UK’s Sustainable Smart Textiles with Integrated Functionality
-------------------------------------------------------------

Researchers from the [University of Cambridge](http://www.eng.cam.ac.uk/news/innovative-wearable-e-textiles-fit-sustainable-circular-economy) advocate for a paradigm shift in e-textile manufacturing towards sustainability, highlighting the need for a transition to unlock innovative designs within a circular economy framework. Embracing the 4R design concept—repair, recycle, replace, reduce—alongside functional fiber production and biofabrication techniques, they propose a revolutionary approach. By integrating these concepts, they aim to address challenges such as high costs and environmental impacts associated with [e-textile](https://textilesinside.com/e-textile-smart-textile/) production.

Their strategy emphasizes material innovations, modular designs, and user-centric approaches to enhance affordability, versatility, and durability. Moreover, they envision a future where e-textiles incorporate self-healing mechanisms, facilitate recycling, and utilize renewable materials, ultimately leading to sustainable and customizable fabric designs. This collaborative effort seeks to redefine e-textile manufacturing, promoting a shift towards decentralized, additive manufacturing platforms for on-site production and repair.

Finnish Ingenuity: Temperature-Reactive Smart Fabrics
-----------------------------------------------------

Researchers at [Aalto University](https://www.aalto.fi/en/news/shrinking-from-the-heat) have developed innovative textiles that undergo shape changes in response to heat, offering designers a plethora of creative possibilities. By utilizing liquid crystalline elastomers (LCEs), which can react to various stimuli, the team has woven these smart materials into fabrics using conventional textile crafting techniques. Collaborating with experts from the University of Cambridge, they explored different weaving patterns and tested how these fabrics respond to heat. The results showed reversible contractions in all LCE fabrics when heated, with each pattern exhibiting a unique response.

Additionally, the team demonstrated a proof-of-concept by weaving LCE yarns with other materials to create a fabric that transforms into a cone when heated and returns to its original shape upon cooling. This interdisciplinary research showcases the potential of integrating advanced materials with traditional textile methods, paving the way for the development of responsive textiles with diverse applications in fashion, healthcare, and interior design.

Techniques, applications, and challenges in textiles for a sustainable future
=============================================================================

Abstract
--------

Next generation textiles (NGTs) represent a paradigm shift in the textile industry, incorporating the most recent advances in materials, technologies, and functionalities. The dynamic world of textiles is undergoing an extraordinary transition, ushering in the age of NGTs. This article discusses the mechanisms, difficulties, present advancements, and potential future opportunities associated with these NGTs. This assessment traverses the domains of nanotechnology, [3D printing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/three-dimensional-printing "Learn more about 3D printing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), recycling, [wearable electronics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wearable-electronics "Learn more about wearable electronics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), machine learning, [biomimicry](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/biomimetics "Learn more about biomimicry from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [energy harvesting](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/energy-harvesting "Learn more about energy harvesting from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") while focusing on sustainability, functionality, smart integration, [advanced manufacturing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/advanced-manufacturing "Learn more about advanced manufacturing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") techniques, and [multifunctionality](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/multifunctionality "Learn more about multifunctionality from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") as major factors. The paper highlights the wonderful prospects of improved performance, sustainability, [intelligent textiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/intelligent-textile "Learn more about intelligent textiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and [wearable technologies](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wearable-technology "Learn more about wearable technologies from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), highlighting consumerization, personalization, safety, and protection enhancements. Furthermore, a key method for overcoming major challenges is discussed which is to adopt open innovation, which encourages collaboration, knowledge exchange, and the integration of external resources, all of which aid in addressing scalability and mass production, costing, and a lack of modern technologies in the NGTs industry.

1. Introduction
---------------

Textiles are positioned to play a significant part in determining the future of fashion, sustainability, and functionality as technology develops and consumer needs change ([Fouda et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib33), [Farooq and Zhang, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib31)). It needs to be more functional, comfortable, responsive as well as sustainable ([Persson et al., 2018](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib81)). The initial purpose of textiles was to protect people from the rain and cold. Later in history, clothes began to incorporate aesthetic considerations as well ([Mostafizur Rahman et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib69)). In recent years, smart and interactive textiles have been introduced as a new generation of [textile fabrics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/textile-fabric "Learn more about textile fabrics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Syduzzaman et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib108)). Next generation textiles (NGTs) represent a paradigm shift in the textile industry, incorporating the most recent advances in materials, technologies, and functionalities. These textiles strive to meet modern demands for sustainability, advanced performance, smart capabilities, and versatility, putting them at the forefront of innovation in the [textile and apparel industries](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/textile-and-apparel-industry "Learn more about textile and apparel industries from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). These textiles go beyond traditional fabrics, incorporating aspects like [smart sensors](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/smart-sensors "Learn more about smart sensors from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), functionality, responsive materials, sustainable components, and functional coatings ([Castano and Flatau, 2014](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib12)). Moreover, the EU took a wonderful initiative to form a strategy that aims to make all textiles sustainable ([Commission, 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib21)).

In recent years, NGTs have become more and more dependent on nanotechnology. Nanotechnology has been used to create fabrics and garments with sustainable antibacterial, UV-resistant, electrically conductive, optical, hydrophobic, and flame-retardant properties ([Hossain et al., 2023a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib42), [Hossain, 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib41)). A wide [range of functions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/range-of-function "Learn more about range of functions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), including [energy harvesting](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/energy-harvesting "Learn more about energy harvesting from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and storage, sensing, drug release, and optics, are now being performed by smart textile-based nanomaterial-based systems. In addition to finding considerable use in the fashion industry, these technologies are being expanded for wider utilization in military, healthcare, and on-body energy harnessing applications ([Shah et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib98)). Another technique is [3D printing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/three-dimensional-printing "Learn more about 3D printing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which can play a wide role in the case of next gen textiles. [3D printing](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/3d-printing "Learn more about 3D printing from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") can be used as stretchable elastic fibers ([Chen et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib15)), and [thermoplastic](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/thermoplastics "Learn more about thermoplastic from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages")[monofilament](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/monofilament "Learn more about monofilament from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") for [smart textile](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/smart-textile "Learn more about smart textile from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), which is flexible and conductive ([Eutionnat-Diffo et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib30)). An article showed about bio-fabricated textile fabrics that were derived from algae, fungi, and other microorganisms. This [bioengineering](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/bioengineering "Learn more about bioengineering from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technique takes the textile fabrics to the next level ([Rognoli et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib86)). Energy-harvesting technology can be incorporated into textiles to power [wearable electronics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wearable-electronics "Learn more about wearable electronics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") and [Internet of Things](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/internet-of-things "Learn more about Internet of Things from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") (IoT) gadgets. Such next-generation fabrics can capture and store energy by energy harvesting technology ([Satharasinghe et al., 2020a](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib95), [Somkuwar et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib104)). Similarly, many technologies have been introduced for NGT, but sustainability is the prime concern ([Nishant et al., 2020](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib73)).

Additionally, it is crucial to ensure that [future textiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/future-textile "Learn more about future textiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") are environmentally friendly. Despite many attempts to be environmentally friendly, some techniques of producing these fabrics can still lead to significant pollution. Nature can be harmed by the way we employ microscopic materials, fabric chemicals, and waste we produce when making these textiles. Another significant area for improvement with employing NGT is safety. Due to its versatility, the product may also introduce electrical dangers, chemical exposure, or heat generation. A difficult issue for advanced textiles is user privacy and security ([Kongahage and Foroughi, 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib51), [Cherenack and Van Pieterson, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib16)).

Several reviews were done regarding the advanced and smart textiles. Cherenack et al. looked at the development, difficulties, design, embedded sensors, device connectivity, and illustrative technologies but should have mentioned the key aspects of it ([Cherenack and Van Pieterson, 2012](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib16)). Meanwhile Farooq et al. examined recent breakthroughs in wearable electronics, focusing on [data transfer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/data-transfer "Learn more about data transfer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), assistive robots, prosthetics, health monitoring, various wearables, and crucial insights regarding the development of electronic technology in the future without mentioning challenges ([Farooq and Zhang, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib32)). In their article, Ramlow and his colleagues addressed the obstacles to widespread commercial production and discussed the developments in [electronic textile](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/electronic-textile "Learn more about electronic textile from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") technologies, energy sources such as energy-harvesting textiles, and [wireless power transfer](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wireless-power-transfer "Learn more about wireless power transfer from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") ([Ramlow et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib84)). Despite the novelty of the technology, Yang et al. discovered that individuals were eager to embrace and employ smart materials and technologies in assistive technology as long as they were comfortable, aesthetically pleasing, and easy to use ([Yang and Moody, 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib123)). In addition, Oliveira and her research team also presented an overview of [intelligent textiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/intelligent-textile "Learn more about intelligent textiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), covering fundamental ideas, classifications, a variety of applications, and an emphasis on important production materials, but not mentioning the cost related to it ([de Oliveira et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib74)). Additionally, Syduzzaman and his colleagues provided a thorough analysis of the advancement of wearable electronics, covering sports, business, healthcare, and more, but they should have mentioned the procedures or techniques used ([Syduzzaman et al., 2015](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib108)).

However, these review papers have not mentioned all the aspects of producing an NGT. This review paper discusses sustainability, functionalization, and other major aspects that are related to NGTs. Also, the obstacle associated with traditional textiles and the chronological development of the textile has illustrated that other review papers did not mention. Considering the research gap, this article demonstrates techniques like Nanotechnology, 3D printing, Recycling, Bioengineering, and so on that are implementable for next-generation textile formation with their challenges and recommendations to make it sustainable. Furthermore, there are a lot of challenges to transforming traditional textiles into advanced textiles, which are not comprehensively referred to by existing review articles. Despite that, probable challenges associated with the transformation of advanced textiles from traditional textiles have been mentioned here. The researchers need to be concerned about the cost, scalability, safety concerns, consumer behavior towards the product, and environmental impact before going through the path. The author has discussed future prospects regarding NGT, such as achieving more sustainability, [customization](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/customisation "Learn more about customization from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and safety features and functionality, in this review paper. This article aims to present a thorough analysis of current textile breakthroughs and trends to pave the way for future technological advancements of NGT by maintaining sustainability toward a greener world.

2. Introduction to textiles
---------------------------

The history of textile development is lengthy and extensive, going back thousands of years. Here is a chronological evolution of textiles unfolding as a magnificent tapestry of innovation and cultural progress across millennia as illustrated in the [Fig. 1](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#fig0005)**.**

![Image 12: Fig. 1](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2199853124000246-gr1.jpg)
1.   [Download: Download high-res image (488KB)](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2199853124000246-gr1_lrg.jpg "Download high-res image (488KB)")
2.   [Download: Download full-size image](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2199853124000246-gr1.jpg "Download full-size image")

Fig. 1. Chronological development of the textiles over the centuries.

From the basic clothing of the prehistoric era to the cutting-edge materials of the 21st century, textiles have continually evolved to human requirements and technical breakthroughs. In ancient civilizations, textiles like linen, cotton, and silk took center stage, establishing cultural identities and global trading routes. The Middle Ages brought in the supremacy of wool, while the Renaissance introduced mechanized looms, promoting industrialization. The 19th century brought the development of synthetic colors and the revolution of [sewing machines](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/sewing-machines "Learn more about sewing machines from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). The 20th century witnessed a synthetic fiber revolution, the emergence of technical textiles, and the rise of digital printing. The 21st century's focus switched to [smart textiles](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/materials-science/smart-textile "Learn more about smart textiles from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), coupling materials with electronics, nanotechnology, and sustainability, pushing the bounds of innovation. This chronological perspective underlines how textiles, woven into the fabric of human history, have continuously adapted to changing times, needs, and opportunities. The historical progression of textiles demonstrates how human creativity and technological progress have continuously altered the manufacture and functionality of fabrics. Textile advances in sustainable practices, innovative materials, and intelligent technology continue to shape the future of the textile business. Additionally, Conventional textiles have a number of urgent concerns, including their considerable environmental effect, production of wasteful practices, dependency on scarce resources, limited usefulness, lack of sustainability, and issues relating to chemical consumption, worker conditions, and consumer awareness. These obstacles demonstrate the need for a more innovative and ecological approach to [textile manufacture](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/textile-processing "Learn more about textile manufacture from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), usage, and disposal. By embracing technological advancements, implementing sustainable practices, and promoting awareness, the textile industry can lessen these problems and create a new opportunity for more responsible and forward-thinking textiles.

3. Key aspect of NGTs
---------------------

Key aspects of NGT have discussed in this section and [Fig. 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#fig0010) illustrated it shortly.

![Image 13: Fig. 2](https://ars.els-cdn.com/content/image/1-s2.0-S2199853124000246-gr2.jpg)

Fig. 2. Schematic diagram on the key aspect of NGTs.

### 3.1. Sustainability

NGT places a high priority on sustainability, using sustainable materials, renewable energy ideas, and responsible manufacturing techniques to reduce their overall environmental effect. In particular, El-Naggar and his coworkers prepared a special cotton swab that can test sweat pH for healthcare and drug testing. They used a substance from a type of cabbage to dye the cotton and make it change color based on pH. The dyeing process didn't affect the fabric's quality and also a sustainable product ([El-Naggar et al., 2021](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib29)). Again, Yin et al. reviewed that advanced textiles may be successfully incorporated during regular wears. It is useful to convert on-body biomechanical, [biochemical](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/biochemicals "Learn more about biochemical from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), and body [heat energy](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/thermal-energy "Learn more about heat energy from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") into electrical signals for long-term, real-time monitoring of [physiological conditions](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/physiological-condition "Learn more about physiological conditions from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"). This can lessen energy consumption and enhance sustainability ([Yin et al., 2023](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib125)). Furthermore, Veeramuthu and colleagues created [wearable electronics](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/wearable-electronics "Learn more about wearable electronics from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that are strong, flexible, and pressure-tolerant, inspired by muscle fibers, suited for [energy harvesting](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/energy-harvesting "Learn more about energy harvesting from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages"), light-up gadgets, and smart gloves, using environmentally friendly manufacturing techniques ([Veeramuthu et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib112)). Meanwhile, Attia et al. reviewed recent developments in the creation of UV-protective coatings for sustainable textiles ([Attia et al., 2022](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib4)).

### 3.2. Functionalization

By using advanced technology, these textiles are designed for certain applications, improving characteristics like water resistance, antibacterial effects, flame resistance, and temperature regulation using additives, coatings, or nanomaterial’s as drawn in [Figure 2](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#fig0010). For example, Quartinello and his research team developed an eco-friendly wound dressing employing cotton/PET blends filled with albumin/silk fibroin [nanocapsules](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/nanocapsule "Learn more about nanocapsules from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") containing natural antimicrobial agents. As it is determined to inhabit the raising of antibiotic resistance in bacteria that has a vital role in environmental safety ([Quartinello et al., 2019](https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2199853124000246#bib82)). Moreover, Zhao et al. used an intensive responsible, and long lasting smart [textile sensor](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/engineering/textile-sensor "Learn more about textile sensor from ScienceDirect's AI-generated Topic Pages") that were produced from flexible nylon thread with [carbon nanotubes](https://www.sciencedirect.com/topics/chemical-engineering/c

## Application of Smart Textiles in Medical and Healthcare

![](https://acmemills.com/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/acme13.png)

Smart textiles:  
Textile industry is one of the oldest industries in the world. Once its purpose was to make only cloth but now a days its purpose is way bigger. New generation of textile industry is called “Smart textile”. Smart textile means those fabrics which have technology woven in them. This technology reacts according to the surrounding and provides data. Application of smart textiles also driven by productivity enhancement and fashion and design purpose. Smart textile is an important branch of technical textile.

Types of smart textile:

Application of smart textile is majorly divided into two important area. They are:

Smart textiles in medical and healthcare field:

If you are thinking that textile is all about garments, fabric, fibers, yarn and making textile goods than you are wrong. Application of smart textile is as much broader as you can imagine. Starting from medical sector to sport it is everywhere. Because of long research and adopting new technologies smart textile industry is booming now a days. In smart textile industry, medical and healthcare is a very important sector.

Read more: [Application of Smart Textiles in Medical and Healthcare](https://www.textileblog.com/application-of-smart-textiles-in-medical-and-healthcare/)

enriched_points.expanded_content: Smart textiles integrate sensors that monitor environmental conditions, physiological data, or user interactions, enabling real-time responses such as temperature regulation or health monitoring.
These textiles are utilized across healthcare, sports, military, and fashion, enhancing performance through features like moisture management, muscle fatigue tracking, and protective gear.

By offering personalized feedback and adaptive functionalities, smart textiles significantly improve user comfort and engagement, transforming everyday clothing into interactive and responsive garments.
